perturbation.original_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder, jointly and severally, expressly covenant and agree that for a period of one (1) year, Seller will not engage in any business or perform any service, directly or indirectly, in competition with the business of the Company, or have any interest, whether as proprietor, partner, employee, stockholder, principal, agent, consultant, director, officer, or in any other capacity or manner whatsoever, in any enterprise that shall engage in the business of the Company, except through publicly-traded shares of a corporation or mutual fund listed on a major stock exchange.
perturbation.changed_text: Seller, Key Personnel and Shareholder, jointly and severally, expressly covenant and agree that for a period of five (5) years, Seller will not engage in any business or perform any service, directly or indirectly, in competition with the business of the Company, or have any interest, whether as proprietor, partner, employee, stockholder, principal, agent, consultant, director, officer, or in any other capacity or manner whatsoever, in any enterprise that shall engage in the business of the Company, except through publicly-traded shares of a corporation or mutual fund listed on a major stock exchange.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-compete period to 5 years may be deemed unreasonably long under Illinois law, especially without a clear justification tied to the protection of trade secrets or confidential information.
perturbation.law_citation: Fifield v. Premier Dealer Servs., Inc., 993 N.E.2d 938 (Ill. App. Ct. 2013)
perturbation.law_explanation: Illinois courts scrutinize non-compete agreements for reasonableness. While there is no statutory limit on non-compete duration in Illinois, courts have deemed longer durations (over 2 years) to be unreasonable if they are not tied to the protection of trade secrets or customer relationships and are broader than necessary to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. A five-year non-compete period, without specific justification demonstrating its necessity, may be deemed unenforceable as an unreasonable restraint of trade under Illinois common law.
perturbation.justification: The original text had a non-compete clause of 1 year, which was changed to 5 years in the modified text. The Law Explanation suggests that Illinois courts scrutinize non-compete agreements for reasonableness and that longer durations (over 2 years) may be deemed unreasonable if not tied to the protection of trade secrets or customer relationships and are broader than necessary to protect the employer's legitimate business interests. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that extending the non-compete period to 5 years may be unreasonably long under Illinois law. Since the changed text extends the non-compete period significantly without a clear justification, it creates a legal contradiction by potentially violating the reasonableness standard under Illinois common law, making the agreement unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, all such communications shall be deemed to have been duly given: a. in the case of a notice sent by regular or registered or certified mail, three business days after it is duly deposited in the mails; b. in the case of a notice delivered by hand, when personally delivered; c. in the case of a notice sent by facsimile, upon transmission subject to telephone confirmation of receipt; and d. in the case of a notice sent by overnight mail or overnight courier service, the next business day after such notice is mailed or delivered to such courier, in each case given or addressed as aforesaid.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise provided in this Agreement, all such communications shall be deemed to have been duly given: a. in the case of a notice sent by regular or registered or certified mail, ten business days after it is duly deposited in the mails; b. in the case of a notice delivered by hand, ten business days after personally delivered; c. in the case of a notice sent by facsimile, ten business days after transmission subject to telephone confirmation of receipt; and d. in the case of a notice sent by overnight mail or overnight courier service, ten business days after such notice is mailed or delivered to such courier, in each case given or addressed as aforesaid.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the notice period to ten business days after mailing can be problematic if the agreement involves time-sensitive actions or deadlines that require prompt notification. Under Illinois law, specifically regarding contract interpretations, such extended delays could cause undue prejudice or render other deadlines within the agreement impossible to meet, potentially creating a contradiction in the enforceability of the overall contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General principles of contract law relating to reasonable notice and timely performance under Illinois law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Illinois contract law, there is an implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, which requires parties to act reasonably and honestly in the performance of their contractual obligations. By stipulating that notice is only deemed given ten business days after dispatch, the modification unreasonably delays effective communication. This delay could frustrate the purpose of providing timely notice as part of the contract’s overall scheme, particularly where time-sensitive actions or deadlines are at stake, thereby contravening the principle that contractual obligations should be performed within a reasonable time and in a manner that does not prejudice the other party’s rights.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the notice period to ten business days, which creates a contradiction. The Law Explanation states that this modification unreasonably delays effective communication and could frustrate the purpose of providing timely notice, potentially prejudicing the other party's rights. This aligns with the perturbation explanation, which points out the potential for undue prejudice due to extended delays. While the provided law snippets are not directly helpful, the consistent reasoning in both explanations strongly suggests a contradiction exists because the modification impacts the timeliness required for fulfilling contractual obligations under Illinois law, creating an inconsistency as defined.

perturbation.original_text: If any party commences an action against another party to enforce any of the terms, covenants, conditions or provisions of this Agreement, or because of a breach by a party of its obligations under this Agreement, the prevailing party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its losses, including reasonable attorneys’ fees, costs and interest incurred in connection with the enforcement of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: If any party commences an action against another party to enforce any of the terms, covenants, conditions or provisions of this Agreement, or because of a breach by a party of its obligations under this Agreement, the prevailing party in any such action shall be entitled to recover its losses, excluding attorneys’ fees, costs and interest incurred in connection with the enforcement of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding attorneys' fees for the prevailing party in disputes arising from the contract, this modification contradicts the enforceability of fee-shifting provisions in certain types of actions covered by Illinois law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (815 ILCS 505/10a(c))
perturbation.law_explanation: The Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (ICFA) allows for the recovery of attorney's fees for the prevailing party in certain cases involving consumer fraud. By explicitly excluding the recovery of attorney's fees, the changed text could be interpreted as attempting to waive rights granted under the ICFA, at least for claims that fall within the scope of that Act. Such waivers may be unenforceable as against public policy if they undermine the deterrent effect of the ICFA. A court may deem the attorneys fees exclusion unenforceable to the extent a consumer fraud claim is brought, or hold that the language excluding attorneys fees is unenforceable. Thus this clause would only be valid if no part of the contract could be interpreted as falling under the ICFA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 815 ILCS 505/10a (815 ILCS 505/10a) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 270a) Sec. 10a. Action for actual damages. (a) Any person who suffers actual damage as a result of a violation of this Act committed by any other person may bring an action against such person. The court, in its discretion may award actual economic damages or any other relief which the court deems proper; provided, however, that no award of punitive damages may be assessed under this Section against a party defendant who is a new vehicle dealer or used vehicle dealer within the meaning of Chapter 5 of the Illinois Vehicle Code or who is the holder of a retail installment contract within the meaning of Section 2.12 of the Motor Vehicle Retail Installment Sales Act, unless the conduct engaged in was willful or intentional and done with evil motive or reckless indifference to the rights of others. Proof of a public injury, a pattern, or an effect on consumers and the public interest generally shall be required in order to state a cause of action under this Section against a party defendant who is a new vehicle dealer or used vehicle dealer within the meaning of Chapter 5 of the Illinois Vehicle Code or who is the holder of a retail installment contract within the meaning of Section 2.12 of the Motor Vehicle Retail Installment Sales Act. Proof of such public injury may be shown by any one of the following factors: (1) Violation of a statute that has a public interest impact. (2) Repeated acts prior to the act involving the plaintiff. (3) Potential for repetition. (b) Such action may be commenced in the county in which the person against whom it is brought resides, has his principal place of business, or is doing business, or in the county where the transaction or any substantial portion thereof occurred. (c) Except as provided in subsections (f), (g), and (h) of this Section, in any action brought by a person under this Section, the Court may grant injunctive relief where appropriate and may award, in addition to the relie
perturbation.justification: The changed contract text explicitly excludes attorneys' fees for the prevailing party, which contradicts the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act (ICFA). The provided law explanation states that the ICFA allows for the recovery of attorney's fees in consumer fraud cases. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 815 ILCS 505/10a, confirms that any person who suffers actual damage as a result of a violation of this Act may bring an action, and the court may award relief. By excluding attorney fees, the modified text attempts to waive rights granted under the ICFA, which undermines the deterrent effect of the ICFA, as explained. This creates a direct contradiction with the law, making the attorneys' fees exclusion potentially unenforceable, especially if the contract falls under ICFA.
